competitor_patterns: Top-ranking pages (OutdoorGearLab, Bikerumor) have extremely high word counts (16,000–24,000 words), combining roundup reviews with deep individual product breakdowns — far exceeding our current 981–1,296 word posts

All top-3 competitors use structured comparison tables and 'Quick Picks' sections at the top, giving users an instant scannable answer before the deep content — our posts currently lack comparison tables entirely

Competitors segment by use case with dedicated H2s (Best MTB, Best Road, Best Value, Best Budget) rather than writing generically about 'cycling glasses' — this captures a wider cluster of long-tail queries from one URL

Every top-ranking competitor page has been tested in real conditions with named reviewers and test dates prominently displayed (e.g. 'Review by Justin Simoni, 5 Jan 2026'), establishing E-E-A-T signals our posts currently do not surface
Competitor pages combine FAQ sections WITH structured review schema, comparison tables, AND star ratings — stacking multiple rich-result triggers simultaneously, whereas our posts only use FAQs
our_gaps: Word count is critically short: our posts average ~1,100 words vs competitors' 16,000–24,000 words — we are not giving Google enough topical depth to compete on broad category terms, and we lack the content mass to internally link a proper topic cluster
Zero comparison tables in any published post — this is a structural miss that hurts both UX and the chance of appearing as a featured snippet or AI Overview answer for 'best X vs Y' queries

No real-world test narrative or named reviewer voice — our posts read as brand-owned marketing copy rather than editorial reviews, which suppresses E-E-A-T signals and makes AI answer engines unlikely to cite us as a trusted source

We have no content targeting MTB/gravel/triathlon sub-segments — all posts address a generic road cyclist, leaving entire intent clusters (gravel glasses, MTB sunglasses, triathlon eyewear) completely uncovered

No external brand mention strategy: competitor roundups derive authority partly from naming and comparing 8–15 brands, which attracts backlinks from those brands' communities — our posts are entirely Velluto-centric with no comparative framework
keyword_opportunities: interchangeable lens cycling glasses — Velo/OutsideOnline ranks #1 here; Velluto's click-in dual-lens system is a direct product match and we have zero content targeting this exact query
best road bike glasses anti fog — anti-fog is a top purchase trigger for sportive and wet-weather riders; our StradaPro has built-in anti-fog but no dedicated content exists for this query
lightweight cycling sunglasses 25g — ultra-specific weight query where our 25g frame is a literal exact match; no competitor has a page optimised around frame weight as the primary differentiator
cycling glasses 30 day trial free returns — purchase-intent query around risk reversal; our 30-day trial is a unique offer and no competitor promotes this as a primary content angle

best cycling glasses for long rides comfort — fit-focused query (adjustable nose pads, temple pressure, weight) that maps perfectly to StradaPro's adjustable nose pads and 25g weight, currently uncovered
next_post_guidelines: Write a minimum of 2,500 words per post — aim for 3,500+ on any pillar/roundup topic; use the extra length to cover buying criteria, use-case segments (road, gravel, commute), care tips, and a detailed product section rather than padding

Open every post with a scannable 'Quick Verdict' comparison table (at minimum 4 rows: Weight / Lens System / Anti-Fog / Price) placed above the first H2 — this directly mirrors the top-ranking content structure and improves featured snippet eligibility

Add a named 'Tested By' byline and a short 'How We Tested' section (even 80 words describing real-ride conditions) to every review-style post — this is the single biggest E-E-A-T signal gap vs OutdoorGearLab and Bikerumor

Structure every roundup or comparison post with use-case H2s: 'Best for Road', 'Best for Gravel', 'Best for Wet Weather', 'Best Budget Option' — each H2 should answer a distinct long-tail query, turning one URL into a multi-keyword asset

For every post about lenses or fit, embed a real product image with descriptive alt text referencing the specific feature discussed (e.g. 'Velluto StradaPro click-in lens swap system') — competitors use heavy visual content that supports dwell time and image search traffic

Target one specific long-tail keyword per post rather than three vague tags — build the entire H1, meta title, first paragraph, and at least two H2s around that single query, then use related terms naturally in body copy

End every post with a CTA that references the 30-day trial explicitly ('Test the StradaPro on your next three rides — free returns if it's not right') — this converts the trust built by long-form content into direct trial sign-ups
seo_quick_wins: Add a structured comparison table (HTML table or styled div) to every post immediately — even a simple 3-column table (Feature / StradaPro / Premium Competitor) is enough to trigger featured-snippet consideration and visually differentiate our content from text-only posts

Implement Review or Article schema markup on all blog posts with 'datePublished', 'author', and 'description' fields populated — this is a zero-cost technical fix that enables rich results (star ratings, dates) in SERPs and signals recency to Google's freshness algorithms

Consolidate post tags to a maximum of 2–3 highly specific tags per post and ensure each tag has its own archive page with a unique meta description — currently tags like 'road cycling' are generic and create thin archive URLs that dilute crawl budget
meta_title_fixes: No GSC data available yet — meta title fixes will be generated once Search Console impressions and CTR data are collected (minimum 28-day data window required). Recommend connecting GSC immediately and re-running this analysis in 4 weeks.
content_quick_wins: No GSC ranking position data available yet — near-top-3 keyword identification requires live Search Console data. Priority action: verify velluto-shop.com in Google Search Console today and submit sitemap to accelerate data collection.
geo_angles: Create a definitive 'Cycling Glasses Buyer's Guide 2026' structured as a Q&A with direct, quotable answers (e.g. 'How heavy should cycling glasses be? Under 30g is the benchmark for comfort on rides over 2 hours — the Velluto StradaPro weighs 25g') — AI answer engines (Perplexity, Google AI Overview) extract concise factual answers from well-structured guides, and specific numbers like '25g' or '30-day trial' are highly citable data points

Publish a comparison post titled 'Oakley Sutro Lite vs Velluto StradaPro: Weight, Lenses & Value Compared' with a clear data table — ChatGPT and Perplexity frequently surface brand-vs-brand comparisons when users ask 'which cycling glasses should I buy', and a structured comparison with specs gives AI models a ready-made answer to cite

Add an 'Expert Summary' box (50–80 words, bold, at the top of each post) that states the key takeaway in one or two punchy sentences — AI Overviews preferentially pull from clearly delineated summary blocks, and including Velluto's specific differentiators (25g, click-in lenses, 30-day trial, UV400) in that box maximises the chance of being cited verbatim in AI-generated answers
comparison_post_angles: COMPETITOR: Outdoorgearlab — angle: 'Why Independent Labs Don't Test Velluto Yet (And What Our Riders Found Instead)' — headline idea: 'Velluto StradaPro vs Oakley Sutro Lite: 25g Real-Ride Test vs Lab Rankings' — key differentiator: independent reviewers test established brands by default; lead with the 30-day trial as Velluto's answer to third-party validation ('test it yourself on your own roads, not in a lab')

COMPETITOR: Bikerumor — angle: 'Julbo Density vs Velluto StradaPro: €260 Premium vs Italian Design at Half the Price' — headline idea: 'Julbo Density vs Velluto StradaPro: Are You Paying for the Logo?' — key differentiator: Bikerumor's #1 pick costs €260; lead with the price-to-feature ratio table showing StradaPro matches on weight and lens interchangeability at a fraction of the cost

COMPETITOR: Tifosi — angle: 'Tifosi Rail vs Velluto StradaPro: Budget Brand vs Premium Feel at a Smarter Price' — headline idea: 'Tifosi Rail vs Velluto StradaPro: Which Interchangeable Lens Glass Wins for Road Riders?' — key differentiator: both brands target value-conscious cyclists; lead with the lens-swap system comparison (Tifosi's mechanism vs Velluto's tool-free click-in) and the Italian design aesthetic as a style differentiator Tifosi cannot match
